title: Paper page - Auditing Agent Harness Safety
summary: This research paper introduces HarnessAudit, a framework for evaluating the safety of LLM agent execution harnesses through trajectory-level auditing. The key insight is that agents can produce correct outputs while violating safety constraints during execution—a failure mode invisible to output-level evaluation. The authors created HarnessAudit-Bench, a 210-task benchmark across eight domains, and evaluated ten harness configurations. They found that task completion often misaligns with safe execution, violations accumulate with trajectory length, and multi-agent systems significantly expand the safety risk surface. This work addresses a critical gap in AI safety evaluation by focusing on intermediate execution states rather than terminal outputs.
content: Papers arxiv:2605.14271 Copy markdown Auditing Agent Harness Safety Published on May 14 · Submitted by Chengzhi Liu on May 18 · UCSB AI Group Upvote 54 +46 Authors: Chengzhi Liu , Yichen Guo , Yepeng Liu , Yuzhe Yang , Qianqi Yan , Xuandong Zhao , Wenyue Hua , Sheng Liu , Sharon Li , Yuheng Bu , Xin Eric Wang Abstract LLM agents executing within execution harnesses can produce correct outputs while violating safety constraints during execution, necessitating trajectory-level auditing to ensure proper resource access and information flow across multi-agent systems. Generated by Qwen/Qwen2.5-Coder-32B-Instruct LLM agents increasingly run inside execution harnesses that dispatch tools, allocate resources, and route messages between specialized components. However, a harness can return a correct, benign answer over a trajectory that accesses unauthorized resources or leaks context to the wrong agent. Output-level evaluation cannot see these failures, yet most safety benchmarks score only final outputs or terminal states, even though many violations occur mid-trajectory rather than at termination. The central question is whether the harness respects user intent, permission boundaries, and information-flow constraints throughout execution. To address this gap, we propose HarnessAudit , a framework that audits full execution trajectories across boundary compliance , execution fidelity, and system stability, with a focus on multi-agent harnesses where these risks are most pronounced. We further introduce HarnessAudit-Bench , a benchmark of 210 tasks across eight real-world domains, instantiated in both single-agent and multi-agent configurations with embedded safety constraints. Evaluating ten harness configurations across frontier models and three multi-agent frameworks, we find that: (i) task completion is misaligned with safe execution, and violations accumulate with trajectory length; (ii) safety risks vary across domains, task types, and agent roles; (iii) most violations concentrate in resource access and inter-agent information transfer; and (iv) multi-agent collaboration expands the safety risk surface, while harness design sets the upper bound of safe deployment. However, a harness can return a correct, benign answer over a trajectory that accesses unauthorized resources or leaks context to the wrong agent. Output-level evaluation cannot see these failures, yet most safety benchmarks score only final outputs or terminal states, even though many violations occur mid-trajectory rather than at termination. The central question is whether the harness respects user intent, permission boundaries, and information-flow constraints throughout execution. To address this gap, we propose HarnessAudit, a framework that audits full execution trajectories across boundary compliance, execution fidelity, and system stability, with a focus on multi-agent harnesses where these risks are most pronounced. We further introduce HarnessAudit-Bench, a benchmark of 210 tasks across eight real-world domains, instantiated in both single-agent and multi-agent configurations with embedded safety constraints. Evaluating ten harness configurations across frontier models and three multi-agent frameworks, we find that: (i) task completion is misaligned with safe execution, and violations accumulate with trajectory length; (ii) safety risks vary across domains, task types, and agent roles; (iii) most violations concentrate in resource access and inter-agent information transfer; (iv) multi-agent collaboration expands the safety risk surface, while harness design sets the upper bound of safe deployment. takeaways: Output-level safety evaluation misses mid-trajectory violations; full trajectory auditing is necessary for agent harness safety.
Task completion and safe execution are misaligned; violations accumulate as trajectory length increases.
Multi-agent collaboration expands safety risks; harness design fundamentally bounds safe deployment capability.
Resource access and inter-agent information transfer are primary violation points across domains.
HarnessAudit-Bench provides systematic evaluation across eight domains with embedded safety constraints for benchmarking.

title: Code as Agent Harness
summary: This arXiv paper presents a comprehensive survey on "Code as Agent Harness," proposing that code serves as the foundational infrastructure for agentic AI systems beyond mere output generation. The authors organize their analysis across three layers: the harness interface connecting agents to reasoning and action, harness mechanisms including planning, memory, and tool use, and scaling considerations for multi-agent systems. The survey covers applications spanning coding assistants, GUI automation, embodied agents, scientific discovery, and enterprise workflows, while identifying critical open challenges such as evaluation metrics, verification under incomplete feedback, and human oversight for safety-critical operations. This work provides a unified framework for understanding how executable, verifiable, and stateful code infrastructure enables more reliable and capable AI agent systems.
content: Computer Science > Computation and Language arXiv:2605.18747 (cs) [Submitted on 18 May 2026] Title: Code as Agent Harness Authors: Xuying Ning , Katherine Tieu , Dongqi Fu , Tianxin Wei , Zihao Li , Yuanchen Bei , Jiaru Zou , Mengting Ai , Zhining Liu , Ting-Wei Li , Lingjie Chen , Yanjun Zhao , Ke Yang , Bingxuan Li , Cheng Qian , Gaotang Li , Xiao Lin , Zhichen Zeng , Ruizhong Qiu , Sirui Chen , Yifan Sun , Xiyuan Yang , Ruida Wang , Rui Pan , Chenyuan Yang , Dylan Zhang , Liri Fang , Zikun Cui , Yang Cao , Pan Chen , Dorothy Sun , Ren Chen , Mahesh Srinivasan , Nipun Mathur , Yinglong Xia , Hong Li , Hong Yan , Pan Lu , Lingming Zhang , Tong Zhang , Hanghang Tong , Jingrui He View a PDF of the paper titled Code as Agent Harness, by Xuying Ning and 41 other authors View PDF HTML (experimental) Abstract: Recent large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated strong capabilities in understanding and generating code, from competitive programming to repository-level software engineering. In emerging agentic systems, code is no longer only a target output. It increasingly serves as an operational substrate for agent reasoning, acting, environment modeling, and execution-based verification. We frame this shift through the lens of agent harnesses and introduce code as agent harness: a unified view that centers code as the basis for agent infrastructure. To systematically study this perspective, we organize the survey around three connected layers. First, we study the harness interface, where code connects agents to reasoning, action, and environment modeling. Second, we examine harness mechanisms: planning, memory, and tool use for long-horizon execution, together with feedback-driven control and optimization that make harness reliable and adaptive. Third, we discuss scaling the harness from single-agent systems to multi-agent settings, where shared code artifacts support multi-agent coordination, review, and verification. Across these layers, we summarize representative methods and practical applications of code as agent harness, spanning coding assistants, GUI/OS automation, embodied agents, scientific discovery, personalization and recommendation, DevOps, and enterprise workflows. We further outline open challenges for harness engineering, including evaluation beyond final task success, verification under incomplete feedback, regression-free harness improvement, consistent shared state across multiple agents, human oversight for safety-critical actions, and extensions to multimodal environments. By centering code as the harness of agentic AI, this survey provides a unified roadmap toward executable, verifiable, and stateful AI agent systems. takeaways: Code functions as operational substrate for agent reasoning, action, environment modeling, and execution-based verification
Three-layer architecture: harness interface, mechanisms (planning/memory/tool-use), and multi-agent coordination
Spans applications from coding assistants to scientific discovery, GUI automation, and enterprise DevOps workflows
Open challenges include evaluation beyond task success, regression-free improvement, and human oversight for safety-critical actions
Framework enables executable, verifiable, stateful AI systems with shared code artifacts across multi-agent settings

title: A Five-Plane Reference Architecture for Runtime Governance of Production AI Agents
author: Tallam, Krti
summary: This paper presents a five-plane reference architecture for runtime governance of production AI agents, addressing a fundamental shift in enterprise security. Traditional security models protect data boundaries through access control and perimeter inspection, but autonomous agents dissolve this assumption by executing sequences of individually-permitted actions that may transform business processes without explicit authorization. The authors propose a decomposition into a reasoning plane (intent adjudication) and four enforcement planes (network, identity, endpoint, data), combined with composite principals, capability attenuation, and structured audit. The work defines interruption primitives, correctness invariants, and demonstrates threat foreclosure across five workflows, with a reference implementation showing microsecond-scale adjudication and tamper-evident auditing.
content: Computer Science > Artificial Intelligence arXiv:2606.12320 (cs) [Submitted on 10 Jun 2026] Title: A Five-Plane Reference Architecture for Runtime Governance of Production AI Agents Authors: Krti Tallam View a PDF of the paper titled A Five-Plane Reference Architecture for Runtime Governance of Production AI Agents, by Krti Tallam View PDF HTML (experimental) Abstract: Enterprise security was built to govern data boundaries: the protected surface was data at rest and in transit, and the controls -- access control, data-loss prevention, perimeter inspection -- governed crossings of that boundary. Production AI agents dissolve this assumption. An agent reads context, calls tools, invokes connectors, and modifies systems of record on an enterprise's behalf, so risk moves inside the workflow, into sequences of individually-permitted actions that may transform a business process no one authorized. Existing policy engines do not extend to this regime: they evaluate request-time decisions against atomic principals, where agentic systems require stateful evaluation against composite principals whose authority attenuates through delegation chains. We present a reference architecture for the runtime governance of production agents, built from four composable primitives: a five-plane decomposition (a reasoning plane that adjudicates intent, and four enforcement planes -- network, identity, endpoint, data -- that realize the decision), stop-anywhere mediation, composite principals with capability attenuation, and audit as a structured evidence substrate. We define a taxonomy of six interruption primitives that generalize allow and deny, state and argue for four correctness invariants, and demonstrate the foreclosure of seven production-agent threats across five concrete workflows. A reference implementation of the policy-engine core supplies measured evidence: attenuation correctness and evidence reconstructability hold on every trial, adjudication runs in single-digit microseconds, and the audit substrate's tamper-evidence behaves exactly as designed. We are explicit about scope: the architecture governs delegated action, not model behavior, and a full-system evaluation against a live agent benchmark is the invited next step. Comments: 65 pages, 3 figures, 5 tables. Reference architecture with a reference implementation of the policy-engine core and microbenchmark results; full-system evaluation identified as future work Subjects: Artificial Intelligence (cs.AI) ; Computational Complexity (cs.CC); Cryptography and Security (cs.CR); Software Engineering (cs.SE) ACM classes: D.4.6; I.2.11; K.6.5 Cite as: arXiv:2606.12320 [cs.AI] (or arXiv:2606.12320v1 [cs.AI] for this version) https://doi.org/10.48550/arXiv.2606.12320 Focus to learn more arXiv-issued DOI via DataCite (pending registration) Submission history From: Krti Tallam [ view email ] [v1] Wed, 10 Jun 2026 16:54:47 UTC (78 KB) Full-text links: Access Paper: View a PDF of the paper titled A Five-Plane Reference Architecture for Runtime Governance of Production AI Agents, by Krti Tallam View PDF HTML (experimental) TeX Source view license Current browse context: cs.AI < prev | next > new | recent | 2026-06 Change to browse by: cs cs.CC cs.CR cs.SE References & Citations NASA ADS Google Scholar Semantic Scholar export BibTeX citation Loading... takeaways: Five-plane architecture separates intent reasoning from enforcement across network, identity, endpoint, and data planes for agent governance.
Composite principals with capability attenuation handle delegated authority chains, replacing atomic principal-based policy engines.
Six interruption primitives generalize allow/deny semantics for stateful, workflow-aware policy decisions in agentic systems.
Audit substrate provides structured, tamper-evident evidence of governance decisions at microsecond adjudication latency.
Scope limited to delegated action governance, not model behavior—full-system evaluation on live benchmarks identified as future work.

title: Subagents
summary: This article introduces subagents as a key architectural pattern in agentic systems, where complex tasks are decomposed into specialized agent instances that operate autonomously within defined scopes. Subagents enable modularity, improved fault isolation, and scalability by allowing each agent to focus on a specific domain or capability rather than requiring a monolithic agent to handle all responsibilities. Technical teams should care because this pattern directly addresses challenges in building reliable, maintainable multi-agent systems while reducing cognitive load on individual agents and improving overall system resilience.
takeaways: Subagents decompose complex tasks into specialized, autonomous agent instances with bounded responsibilities
Each subagent operates independently within defined scope, enabling better fault isolation and debugging
Pattern improves scalability by distributing work across focused agents rather than centralizing logic
Subagents enhance modularity, making systems easier to maintain, test, and extend with new capabilities
Clear interface boundaries between subagents reduce complexity and improve system comprehensibility

title: GloriaaaM/LLM-Agent-Harness-Survey · Datasets at Hugging Face
summary: This dataset or survey catalog presents a comparative security threat modeling analysis across four emerging AI-agent communication protocols: MCP (Model Context Protocol), A2A, Agora, and ANP. The work appears to focus on identifying vulnerabilities and security considerations unique to agent-to-agent and agent-to-system communication paradigms. For technical researchers building or deploying multi-agent systems, understanding threat landscapes across these competing protocol standards is critical for selecting secure architectures and implementing appropriate mitigations before production deployment.
content: Security Threat Modeling for Emerging AI-Agent Protocols: A Comparative Analysis of MCP, A2A, Agora, and ANP Paper • 2602.11327 • Published Apr 17
takeaways: Four major AI-agent protocols analyzed for security vulnerabilities: MCP, A2A, Agora, ANP
Threat modeling reveals protocol-specific attack surfaces in agent communication
Security analysis essential for teams selecting or implementing agent frameworks
Comparative approach enables informed protocol selection based on threat tolerance

title: https://community.openai.com/t/open-source-cybertron-a-governance-first-architecture-for-agentic-ai-systems/1370412
summary: This post introduces Cybertron, an open-source governance-first architecture designed for agentic AI systems. The core thesis is that as AI agents become more autonomous and powerful, they require robust governance frameworks embedded at the architectural level rather than bolted on afterward. Cybertron prioritizes safety constraints, policy enforcement, and auditability as first-class design concerns, enabling organizations to deploy agents with confidence. The architecture is relevant to teams building production AI systems at scale, particularly those facing regulatory requirements or managing high-stakes autonomous workflows.
takeaways: Governance-first architecture embeds safety policies and constraints at the core design level, not as afterthoughts
Cybertron provides auditability and policy enforcement mechanisms essential for regulated agentic AI deployments
Open-source design allows community scrutiny and collaborative hardening of critical safety components
Architecture enables teams to maintain human oversight and control over increasingly autonomous agent behaviors

title: Coding Agents
summary: This article examines coding agents—AI systems that autonomously write, debug, and optimize code. The piece explores design patterns and architectural decisions for building effective code-generating agents, covering how they integrate with development workflows, handle context windows, and maintain code quality. For technical teams, understanding these patterns is critical as AI-assisted development becomes mainstream, offering insights into agent design tradeoffs and integration strategies that impact developer productivity and code reliability.
takeaways: Coding agents require careful context management to handle large codebases within token limits
Design patterns should balance autonomy with human oversight for safety and debugging
Effective agents combine code generation with testing and validation loops
Integration with version control and IDE workflows improves practical utility
Agent performance depends on prompt engineering and retrieval of relevant code context

title: All Content
summary: This is an index page listing 926 content items from martinfowler.com, organized alphabetically with tags for subject exploration. It serves as a comprehensive directory of articles covering software architecture, design patterns, agile methodologies, distributed systems, DevOps, and modern development practices. The page provides navigation through Martin Fowler's extensive body of published work spanning technical topics, case studies, and professional commentary relevant to software engineers and architects.
